senses.gloss: a game of chance of drawing lots from arrows with typically seven participants for the shares in an allotted prize (usually a camel’s meat)

examples.text: يَسْأَلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلْخَمْرِ وَٱلْمَيْسِرِ ۖ قُلْ فِيهِمَا إِثْمٌ كَبِيرٌ وَمَنَافِعُ لِلنَّاسِ وَإِثْمُهُمَا أَكْبَرُ مِن نَفْعِهِمَا ۗ

يَا أَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّمَا ٱلْخَمْرُ وَٱلْمَيْسِرُ وَٱلْأَنْصَابُ وَٱلْأَزْلَامُ رِجْسٌ مِنْ عَمَلِ ٱلشَّيْطَانِ فَٱجْتَنِبُوهُ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ ۝ إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ ٱلشَّيْطَانُ أَن يُوقِعَ بَيْنَكُمُ ٱلْعَدَاوَةَ وَٱلْبَغْضَاءَ فِي ٱلْخَمْرِ وَٱلْمَيْسِرِ وَيَصُدَّكُمْ عَن ذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ وَعَنِ ٱلصَّلَاةِ ۖ فَهَلْ أَنْتُم مُنْتَهُونَ

senses.gloss: gambling (because of generalizing the Qurʾān occurrences)

etymology.text: Derived from the passive participle of يَسَّرَ (yassara, “to make easy”).

body.text: play, with unfeathered and headless arrows;
game of hazard which the Arabs play with such arrows;
a game of the Arabs, played
with ten unfeathered and headless arrows:

they first slaughtered a camel, [bought on credit, (see below, in this paragraph,)] and divided it into ten portions, or, as some say, [agreeably with what follows,] into twenty-eight: the first arrow was called
, and had [one notch and] one portion of the slaughtered camel: the second,
, and had [two notches and] two portions: the third,
, and had [three notches and] three portions: the fourth,
, and had [four notches and] four portions: the fifth,
, and had [five notches and] five portions; or, as some say, this was the fourth: the sixth,
, and had [six notches and] six portions: the seventh,
, which was the highest of them, having [seven notches and] seven portions: the eighth and ninth and tenth were called
; and these three had no portions: [the players to whom these three fell had to pay for the slaughtered camel: (see
:) whence it appears, that if the camel was divided into ten portions, (see

,) the game must have continued after all these were won, until it was seen whose were the eighth and ninth and tenth arrows; and it seems to be the general opinion that this was the case:] the camel being slaughtered, they collected together the ten arrows, and put them into the
), and turned them round about or shuffled them (
): [or they employed a person, whom they called
, to do this:] then they put them into the hand of the judge (
), who took them forth one after another in the name of one after another of the party; [or they commissioned the

to do so;] and each took of the portions of the slaughtered camel according to his arrow; but those to whose lots fell the arrows without portions were obliged to pay the price of the slaughtered camel: with the flesh of which they afterwards fed the poor; and him who would not engage with them in the game they reproached, and called a
: (Sefeenet Er-Rághib, printed at Boolák; p. 637:) [see also
(K:) so that even the game of children with walnuts is included under this name by Mujáhid in his explanation of verse 216 of chap. ii. of the Kur.: (TA:) or
anything in which is risk, or hazard:
of the Persians, or foreigners: (TA:) or the
slaughtered camel for which they played:

for when they desired to play, they bought on credit a camel for slaughter, and slaughtered it, and divided it into twentyeight portions, or ten portions; and when one [of the arrows] after another came forth [from the
] in the name of one man after another, the gain of him for whom came forth those to which belonged portions appeared, and the fine of him for whom came forth [any of the arrows called] the
: (K:) so called as though it were a place of division: and so used by the poet Lebeed, who speaks of a fat
So in the following words of a trad.:
for that for which he is created
[q. v.]; (Mgh, K;) app. a post-classical word; so called because easily taken; (Mgh;) in Persian, called
